areas.title: Public launch and repo hygiene
areas.label: area:public-launch
acceptanceSignals: No internal-only or private/IP content remains in tracked public-facing docs and metadata
Support, contribution, and security routes are obvious to external users
Public repo messaging matches the released Gofer product
reviewQuestions: Does this actually remove public-risk material instead of only hiding it?
Does it keep README, docs site, releases, and repo metadata consistent?

areas.title: Onboarding and docs
areas.label: area:docs
acceptanceSignals: A new user can reach a successful first run quickly
Docs are consistent across README, docs site, and host-specific install instructions
Examples are concrete and copy-paste friendly
reviewQuestions: Does this reduce first-run confusion across VS Code and CLI hosts?
Is the documentation source-of-truth updated rather than only a generated mirror?

areas.title: Cross-host install/update parity
areas.label: area:host-parity
acceptanceSignals: Install and update paths are correct for each supported host
Generated command surfaces stay aligned across hosts
Workspace bootstrap behavior is clear and testable
reviewQuestions: Does the change improve the real install/update behavior, not just the docs?
Are generated manifests and mirrored surfaces still in sync?

areas.title: Release and packaging
areas.label: area:release
acceptanceSignals: Release automation produces the expected public artifacts
Marketplace/manifests stay aligned with the released version
Public release metadata is deterministic and verifiable
reviewQuestions: Does this keep release automation source-of-truth in one place?
Will the next release preserve the same public behavior without manual repair?

areas.title: Community and contributor experience
areas.label: area:community
acceptanceSignals: External contributors know where to ask questions and where to file issues
Support and security routing are explicit
Community automation reinforces the public roadmap instead of creating noise
reviewQuestions: Does this make contributor expectations more obvious?
Does it reduce maintainer triage overhead without blocking legitimate issues?

areas.title: Validation and CI quality
areas.label: area:quality
acceptanceSignals: Automated checks catch the intended regression before release
Public-facing defaults and workflow messages are covered by tests
CI comments and artifacts help reviewers, not just maintainers
reviewQuestions: Does this improve confidence in public releases or contributor changes?
Is the behavior covered by a durable automated check?
